standards.standard_description: Refer to details and examples in a text when explaining what the text says explicitly and when drawing inferences.
standards.parent_tip: Ask your child to support their answers with evidence: 'What in the story makes you think that?'

questions.prompt:
Elena clutched her telescope and crept onto the rooftop. The city lights dimmed the stars, but she knew exactly where to look. At 9:47 PM, a streak of light raced across the sky — the comet her grandfather had told her about.

What can you infer about Elena's grandfather?
choices: He shared his knowledge of astronomy with Elena
He has never seen a comet
questions.correct: He shared his knowledge of astronomy with Elena
questions.hint: Elena knew exactly where to look because her grandfather told her about the comet.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The story says Grandpa told Elena about the comet, so we can tell he shared what he knew about the stars with her.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, think about who told Elena about the comet in the first place. What does that say about him?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is that he shared his knowledge of astronomy with Elena. She knew about the comet because Grandpa taught her.

questions.prompt:
The letter had been folded and refolded so many times that the creases were soft as fabric. Marcus kept it in his front pocket, close to his chest, even though he'd memorized every word.

What can you infer about how Marcus feels about the letter?
choices: He wants to throw it away
It's extremely important and meaningful to him
questions.correct: It's extremely important and meaningful to him
questions.hint: He keeps it close to his chest and has memorized every word — those are signs of deep attachment.
questions.correct_feedback: Exactly! He memorized every word and keeps it close to his chest. Those details show the letter means so much to him.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost. Why would someone keep a letter close and read it until it's soft and worn? What does that show?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is that it's extremely important and meaningful to him. He keeps it close and knows every word by heart.

questions.prompt:
Priya practiced her free throws every morning before school and every evening after dinner. On the day of tryouts, she was the only one who made all ten shots.

What detail from the text BEST supports the idea that hard work pays off?
choices: She practiced before school and after dinner
questions.correct: She practiced before school and after dinner
questions.hint: The detail about her practice schedule directly explains why she succeeded at tryouts.
questions.correct_feedback: Nice thinking! Practicing before school and after dinner shows all the hard work that helped Priya make every shot.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try. Which detail actually shows Priya putting in effort over and over, not just something she did once?
questions.reveal_feedback: The best answer is that she practiced before school and after dinner. That steady effort is what made her hard work pay off.

questions.prompt:
Dad hadn't said a word during the entire drive home. He gripped the steering wheel tightly, and his jaw was clenched. When they pulled into the driveway, he finally sighed and said, "We'll talk about this inside."

What can you infer about Dad's mood?
choices: He's upset or frustrated about something
questions.correct: He's upset or frustrated about something
questions.hint: Silent, tight grip, clenched jaw, deep sigh — those are all signs of frustration.
questions.correct_feedback: You got it! The clenched jaw, tight grip, and silence are all clues that Dad is upset or frustrated about something.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one. Picture Dad's tight grip, clenched jaw, and silence. Do those clues sound like a good mood or a bad one?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is that he's upset or frustrated about something. His clenched jaw and silence give his feelings away.

questions.prompt:
The old bookshop smelled of leather and dust. Mrs. Tanaka knew every book by heart — where it sat, what it was about, who had loved it last. When the city announced the building would be torn down, she simply said, "Books always find a new home."

What does Mrs. Tanaka's response reveal about her character?
choices: She's optimistic and resilient even in difficult times
She doesn't care about the bookshop
questions.correct: She's optimistic and resilient even in difficult times
questions.hint: Instead of being devastated, she focuses on the positive — books will survive. That's resilience.
questions.correct_feedback: Well done! Even as her shop is torn down, she says books will find a new home. That shows she's optimistic and resilient.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet. Think about her calm, hopeful words even when she's losing the shop. What does that reveal about her?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is that she's optimistic and resilient even in difficult times. She stays hopeful even as the shop is torn down.

questions.prompt: Maria was the fastest runner on her team. When the coach needed someone to run the final leg of the relay, he always picked Maria. Why did the coach pick Maria for the final leg?
choices: Because she was the fastest runner on the team
Because she did not like running
Because she was the tallest student
questions.correct: Because she was the fastest runner on the team
questions.hint: Look at the first sentence. It tells you exactly what made Maria special.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The story says the coach always picked Maria because she was the fastest runner on the team.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. The story tells you one special thing about Maria's running. What made her stand out?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is that she was the fastest runner on the team. That's exactly why the coach chose her for the final leg.

questions.prompt: The rain fell all day, so the baseball game was canceled. The players went home disappointed. Why was the game canceled?
choices: Because the players won a prize
Because the field was too sunny
questions.hint: The word 'so' connects the cause to what happened next. Find the reason right before it.
questions.correct_feedback: That's right! The story says the rain fell all day, and that's the reason the game was canceled.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, look back at the weather in the story. What was happening all day long?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is that it rained all day. The rain is what caused the game to be canceled.

questions.prompt: Ben studied his spelling words every night for a week. On Friday, he got every word right on the test. What detail shows that Ben worked hard?
choices: He studied his spelling words every night for a week
questions.correct: He studied his spelling words every night for a week
questions.hint: Find the sentence that tells what Ben did each night before the test.
questions.correct_feedback: Great! Studying his spelling words every night for a week is the detail that shows Ben really worked hard.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try. Which detail shows Ben putting in real effort, not goofing off or skipping?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is that he studied his spelling words every night for a week. That steady practice shows his hard work.

questions.prompt: When the puppy heard the front door open, it wagged its tail and ran to greet its owner. How does the puppy feel about its owner?
choices: Happy and excited to see them
questions.correct: Happy and excited to see them
questions.hint: Think about what a wagging tail and running to greet someone usually shows.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Wagging its tail and running to greet its owner shows the puppy is happy and excited to see them.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost. Think about a wagging tail and running to say hello. Do those actions seem happy or upset?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is that the puppy feels happy and excited to see them. The wagging tail gives it away.

questions.prompt: Grandpa handed Lily an old baseball glove. 'This was mine when I was your age,' he said with a smile. What can you tell about the glove?
choices: It has belonged to Grandpa since he was young
Lily bought it at the store today
questions.correct: It has belonged to Grandpa since he was young
questions.hint: Grandpa's own words tell you who the glove used to belong to.
questions.correct_feedback: Nice! Grandpa says the glove was his when he was Lily's age, so it has belonged to him since he was young.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not quite. Grandpa said the glove was his 'when I was your age.' What does that tell you about how old it is?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is that it has belonged to Grandpa since he was young. He had it back when he was Lily's age.

standards.standard_description: Determine a theme of a story, drama, or poem from details in the text; summarize the text.
standards.parent_tip: Ask 'What big life lesson does this story teach?' — that's the theme.

questions.prompt:
Kai wanted to win the science fair more than anything. He worked alone, refusing help from his classmates. His project looked impressive but had a major flaw he hadn't noticed. Meanwhile, the team next to him — who had shared ideas and caught each other's mistakes — won first place.

What is the theme?
choices: Collaboration leads to better results than working alone
questions.correct: Collaboration leads to better results than working alone
questions.hint: Compare what happened to Kai (alone, missed a flaw) vs. the team (shared ideas, won).
questions.correct_feedback: Exactly! The team that shared ideas and caught mistakes won, showing collaboration leads to better results than working alone.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. Compare Kai working alone to the team that shared ideas. Which one ended up doing better?
questions.reveal_feedback: The theme is that collaboration leads to better results than working alone. The team that helped each other won first place.

questions.prompt:
Amara was embarrassed by her grandmother's accent and old-fashioned clothes. She asked Grandma not to come to the school play. After the show, Amara saw her classmates laughing with their grandparents and felt a deep ache. She called Grandma that night and said, "Next time, front row."

What is the theme?
choices: Don't be ashamed of your family — appreciate them
questions.correct: Don't be ashamed of your family — appreciate them
questions.hint: Amara regretted pushing her grandmother away and realized family matters more than appearances.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Amara felt an ache when she saw others with their grandparents. The lesson is don't be ashamed of your family, appreciate them.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one. Amara felt sad after leaving Grandma out, then invited her back. What big lesson did she learn?
questions.reveal_feedback: The theme is don't be ashamed of your family, appreciate them. Amara learned this after regretting leaving Grandma out.

questions.prompt:
Read the story below. What is the main lesson the story about Lily teaches?

Lily wanted to learn to ride her bike without training wheels. She fell many times. Her knees got scraped, and she felt like giving up. But her dad told her, "Keep trying, Lily! You'll get it!" Lily took a deep breath. She got back on the bike. She pushed off and wobbled. Then, she pedaled a little further. After many more tries, she finally rode all the way down the sidewalk without falling! A big smile spread across her face.
choices: It is important to have a bike.
Never give up, even when things are hard.
Trying new things is always easy.
questions.correct: Never give up, even when things are hard.
questions.hint: Think about what Lily did after she kept falling and how she felt when she finally succeeded in riding her bike.
questions.correct_feedback: Great! Lily fell many times but kept trying until she rode the bike. The lesson is never give up, even when things are hard.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, think about what Lily did after all her falls and scraped knees. What big idea does her story teach?
questions.reveal_feedback: The lesson is never give up, even when things are hard. Lily kept trying after every fall until she succeeded.

questions.prompt:
The tiny seed was carried by the wind past tall trees and mighty oaks. It landed in a crack between two rocks where nothing grew. But the seed pushed through anyway, and years later it became the only tree on that rocky hill.

What is the theme?
choices: Determination can help you thrive even in difficult conditions
questions.correct: Determination can help you thrive even in difficult conditions
questions.hint: The seed grew where 'nothing grew' — it succeeded against the odds through persistence.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The seed grew in a rocky crack where nothing else could. The theme is determination can help you thrive in difficult conditions.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost. The seed grew where nothing else could survive. What bigger idea about not giving up does that show?
questions.reveal_feedback: The theme is that determination can help you thrive even in difficult conditions. The seed pushed through against all odds.

questions.prompt: Read the story about Lily.Lily the squirrel woke up feeling very hungry in her cozy nest. She remembered a big, shiny acorn on the ground. When Lily scurried down the tree to get it, a grumpy blue jay was already pecking at her acorn! Lily didn't give up easily. She puffed up her fur and stamped her tiny feet, making the blue jay squawk and fly away. Happy, Lily grabbed her acorn and carried it back up to her nest for a yummy breakfast.Which sentence best summarizes the story?
choices: A grumpy blue jay was pecking at an acorn, but then it flew away.
Lily's nest was cozy, and she saw a shiny acorn on the ground near an old fence.
Lily the squirrel was hungry, so she bravely scared away a blue jay to get a yummy acorn for breakfast.
Lily woke up feeling hungry and scurried down the tree to get an acorn.
questions.correct: Lily the squirrel was hungry, so she bravely scared away a blue jay to get a yummy acorn for breakfast.
questions.hint: To find the best summary, think about the main character, what problem she faced, and how she solved it throughout the entire story.
questions.correct_feedback: Perfect! That sentence covers the whole story: hungry Lily bravely scared away the blue jay to get her acorn breakfast.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try. A summary covers the WHOLE story from start to finish, not just one small part. Which one does that?
questions.reveal_feedback: The best summary is that Lily the squirrel was hungry, so she bravely scared away a blue jay to get her acorn breakfast. It covers the whole story.

standards.standard_description: Describe in depth a character, setting, or event, drawing on specific details in the text.
standards.parent_tip: Ask your child to describe characters using evidence: 'What did they say or do that shows that trait?'

questions.prompt:
The marketplace buzzed with voices in a dozen languages. Spices piled in colorful mountains — saffron yellow, paprika red, turmeric orange. Somewhere a radio played scratchy music, and the smell of roasting lamb drifted through the narrow aisles.

The author uses these details to create a setting that feels —
choices: Vibrant, busy, and full of sensory experiences
questions.correct: Vibrant, busy, and full of sensory experiences
questions.hint: Voices, colors, music, smells — the author hits sight, sound, and smell to bring it alive.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The many languages, colorful spices, music, and smells make the setting feel vibrant, busy, and full of sensory experiences.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. Sounds, smells, colors, and crowds fill this scene. What kind of feeling do all those details create?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is vibrant, busy, and full of sensory experiences. The sounds, smells, and colors bring the marketplace alive.

questions.prompt:
Jordan's hands trembled as he opened the envelope. His eyes scanned the page, then widened. He read it again. Then a third time. Then he let out a whoop so loud the neighbors" dog started barking.

What can you infer about the letter's contents?
questions.hint: Trembling hands (nervous), then widened eyes and a whoop (excitement) — that's great news!
questions.correct_feedback: You got it! Reading it three times and letting out a happy whoop are clues that the letter held very good news.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one. Jordan reads it again and again, then whoops with joy. Does that sound like good news or bad?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is very good news. Jordan's excited whoop and re-reading show he was thrilled by the letter.

questions.prompt: Read the passage about Leo. Then, based on the passage, which statement best describes Leo in depth? Leo was the new kid in Mrs. Davis's class. He often wore a blue shirt and had bright, curious eyes behind his round glasses. During recess, while other kids ran and played tag, Leo found a quiet spot under the big oak tree. He carefully opened a thick book about dinosaurs, his favorite topic. When Maya walked over and asked, 'What are you reading?' Leo looked up, a small smile appearing on his face, and eagerly began to explain the different types of dinosaurs.
choices: Leo is a new student who likes to wear a blue shirt and has round glasses.
Leo is a shy but friendly new student who loves to read, especially about dinosaurs, and shows this by reading quietly at recess and sharing his interest with others.
Leo is a new student who enjoys reading books about dinosaurs.
Leo likes to play tag with his friends during recess.
questions.correct: Leo is a shy but friendly new student who loves to read, especially about dinosaurs, and shows this by reading quietly at recess and sharing his interest with others.
questions.hint: To describe Leo in depth, look at his actions, his reactions, and what he chooses to do when he has free time in the passage.
questions.correct_feedback: Excellent! The best answer captures it all: Leo is shy but friendly, loves dinosaurs, reads quietly, then shares his interest with Maya.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, describing Leo 'in depth' means using several clues about who he is, not just one. Which answer does the most?
questions.reveal_feedback: The best answer is that Leo is a shy but friendly new student who loves reading, especially dinosaurs, and shares his interest with others. It uses the most clues.

questions.prompt:
The abandoned lighthouse stood at the edge of the cliff, its paint peeling like sunburned skin. Waves crashed against the rocks below, sending spray twenty feet into the air. Inside, the spiral staircase groaned under the weight of years.

The setting creates a mood that is —
questions.hint: Abandoned, peeling paint, crashing waves, groaning staircase — all create a lonely, spooky mood.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Peeling paint, crashing waves, and a groaning staircase all build a mood that's eerie and isolated.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet. Picture an abandoned lighthouse alone on a cliff with groaning stairs. What mood does that give you?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is eerie and isolated. The abandoned lighthouse and crashing waves create a lonely, spooky mood.

questions.prompt: Maria was the kindest kid in class. She shared her lunch, helped others clean up, and always said something nice. What kind of person is Maria?
questions.hint: Look at what Maria does: she shares, helps, and says nice things. What word matches those actions?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Sharing her lunch, helping clean up, and saying nice things all show Maria is kind and helpful.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. Maria shares, helps, and says nice things. What kind of person acts that way?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is kind and helpful. Maria shares her lunch and helps others, which shows exactly that.

questions.prompt: The old house was dark and dusty. Cobwebs hung in every corner, and the floors creaked with each step. What is the setting like?
questions.hint: Think about the dust, cobwebs, and creaky floors. Do those details feel new and cheerful, or old and creepy?
questions.correct_feedback: That's right! Darkness, dust, cobwebs, and creaky floors make the house feel spooky and old.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, imagine a dark house full of cobwebs with creaky floors. Does that feel cheerful or something else?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is spooky and old. The cobwebs, dust, and creaky floors all point to that setting.

questions.prompt: Ben pumped his fist in the air and shouted, "We won!" He jumped up and down with a huge smile. How does Ben feel?
questions.hint: Ben is jumping, shouting, and smiling big. What feeling do those actions show?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Pumping his fist, shouting 'We won,' and jumping with a huge smile show Ben feels excited and happy.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost. Ben shouts 'We won' and jumps with a big smile. Do those actions sound happy or upset?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is excited and happy. Ben's cheering and big smile show his feelings clearly.

questions.prompt: The beach was warm and sunny. Waves splashed softly, and kids built sandcastles while seagulls flew above. What is the setting like?
questions.hint: Picture the warm sun, splashing waves, and sandcastles. Does that place sound sunny and fun, or cold and scary?
questions.correct_feedback: Great! Warm sun, splashing waves, and sandcastles make this beach setting sunny and fun.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try. Think about warm sun, gentle waves, and kids building sandcastles. What is that setting like?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is sunny and fun. The warm beach and playing kids create that cheerful setting.

questions.prompt: Every morning, Grandpa woke up early to feed the chickens, fix the fence, and water the garden. He never stopped working. What kind of person is Grandpa?
questions.hint: Grandpa feeds, fixes, and waters and never stops. What word describes someone who works this much?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Feeding chickens, fixing fences, and never stopping show that Grandpa is hardworking.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not quite. Grandpa wakes early and never stops working on his chores. What kind of person is that?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is hardworking. Grandpa's early mornings and endless chores show it well.

standards.standard_description: Determine the meaning of words and phrases as used in a text, including those that allude to significant characters found in mythology.
standards.parent_tip: Discuss how authors use words creatively — 'What does the author really mean by that phrase?'

questions.prompt:
She had the Midas touch — every business she started turned into gold.

This allusion to King Midas means —
choices: She literally turned things to gold
questions.hint: King Midas turned everything to gold. This phrase means everything she touches succeeds.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The Midas touch means whatever she did turned to 'gold,' so everything she did became successful.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. King Midas turned things to gold, but here it's used as a comparison. What does 'turning to gold' really mean for her business?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is that everything she did became successful. The Midas touch is a way of saying her work always turned out golden.

questions.prompt:
Cleaning out the garage was a Herculean task.

This allusion to Hercules means the task was —
choices: Extremely difficult and required great effort
questions.correct: Extremely difficult and required great effort
questions.hint: Hercules was famous for completing incredibly difficult tasks (his twelve labors).
questions.correct_feedback: Right! Hercules did mighty, tough labors, so a Herculean task is one that's extremely difficult and needs great effort.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, Hercules was famous for his hardest challenges. So what kind of task would a 'Herculean' one be?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is extremely difficult and required great effort. A Herculean task is a huge, tough job, just like Hercules faced.

questions.prompt:
The morning light tiptoed through the curtains.

What does **tiptoed** suggest about the light?
choices: It was very bright and harsh
questions.hint: Tiptoeing is quiet and careful — the light came in softly, not harshly.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Light can't really tiptoe, but the word makes it feel like the light entered gently and softly.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one. Think about how a person tiptoes into a room. How would light 'tiptoe' through the curtains?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is that it entered gently and softly. 'Tiptoed' makes the morning light seem quiet and careful, like a person on tiptoe.

questions.prompt:
His Achilles" heel was his temper — it always got him into trouble.

An **Achilles** heel" refers to —
choices: A person's greatest weakness or vulnerability
questions.correct: A person's greatest weakness or vulnerability
questions.hint: In mythology, Achilles was invincible except for his heel. The phrase means a fatal weakness.
questions.correct_feedback: Exactly! An Achilles' heel is a person's greatest weakness or vulnerability, just like his temper was his.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. It's not really about his foot. If his temper always got him in trouble, what does 'Achilles heel' stand for?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is a person's greatest weakness or vulnerability. His temper was the one weak spot that kept tripping him up.

questions.prompt:
The silence in the library was so thick you could cut it with a knife.

This phrase means —
choices: People were cooking in the library
Someone had a knife in the library
The silence was intense and heavy
questions.correct: The silence was intense and heavy
questions.hint: This is figurative — the silence felt so strong and present it was almost physical.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! You can't really cut silence with a knife. It means the silence was so intense and heavy you could almost feel it.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet. Nobody's really using a knife here. What is the phrase saying about how strong the silence felt?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is that the silence was intense and heavy. Saying you could 'cut it with a knife' means it felt thick and powerful.

questions.prompt:
The knight was as brave as a lion, so he was not scared at all when the dragon roared.

In this sentence, "as brave as a lion" means the knight was —
questions.hint: The sentence says he was "not scared at all." A lion is known for being brave.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Lions are known for courage, so 'as brave as a lion' means the knight was very brave.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, what are lions famous for? That's the quality the knight has when facing the dragon.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is very brave. Comparing the knight to a lion means he was full of courage.

questions.prompt:
The old wizard had a booming voice. It was so loud and deep that it filled the whole cave.

In this sentence, a "booming" voice means a voice that is —
questions.hint: Read the next sentence: it says the voice was "so loud and deep."
questions.correct_feedback: Right! The story says the voice was loud and deep enough to fill the cave, so 'booming' means loud and deep.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. A booming voice fills the whole cave. Does that sound tiny and soft, or something bigger?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is loud and deep. A booming voice is big and powerful, like thunder filling the cave.

questions.prompt:
Maya felt gloomy all day. She frowned and did not want to play or smile.

In this sentence, "gloomy" means Maya felt —
questions.hint: She frowned and did not want to smile. How does someone feel when they act that way?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Maya frowned and didn't want to play or smile, so 'gloomy' means she felt sad.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost. Maya frowned and didn't want to smile or play. What feeling matches those actions?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is sad. 'Gloomy' describes Maya's down, frowning mood.

questions.prompt:
Ben's little sister followed him everywhere, so he called her his shadow because she was always right behind him.

Calling his sister his "shadow" means she —
questions.hint: The sentence tells you why: she was "always right behind him."
questions.correct_feedback: Great! A shadow is always right behind you, so calling his sister his 'shadow' means she always stayed close behind him.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try. She isn't really made of darkness. Think about where a shadow goes. What is the sister doing?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is that she always stayed close behind him. Like a real shadow, she followed him everywhere.

questions.prompt:
The hero was so strong he could lift a giant boulder. People said he had the strength of Hercules, a mighty hero from Greek myths.

Saying the hero had "the strength of Hercules" means he was very —
questions.hint: The sentence tells you Hercules was a "mighty hero" and the hero could lift a giant boulder.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Hercules was a mighty hero, so having 'the strength of Hercules' means the hero was very strong.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, Hercules was famous for one amazing ability, and the hero could lift a boulder. What quality is that?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is strong. Comparing him to Hercules shows just how much power the hero had.

standards.standard_description: Explain major differences between poems, drama, and prose; refer to structural elements when writing or speaking about a text.
standards.parent_tip: Compare how the same story might look as a poem, a play, and a chapter book.

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: Maya wrote a play for her class. Part of her script read: MAYA (waving her arms): Wait for me! [She runs across the stage and trips over a chair.] Later, Maya rewrote the very same scene as a regular story: 'Maya waved her arms and shouted for her friends to wait. Then she ran and tripped over a chair.' In the play, the notes inside the brackets told the actors exactly how to move, but the regular story just described everything in plain sentences. Which structural element is found in a drama (play) but NOT in prose (a regular story)?
questions.hint: Stage directions (in italics or brackets) tell actors how to move and speak — only plays have these.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The notes in brackets that tell actors how to move are stage directions, which plays have but regular stories don't.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. Which element told the actors exactly how to move? Chapters and paragraphs show up in stories too.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is stage directions. Those bracketed notes telling actors how to move are found in plays but not in regular stories.

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: Ben wrote the same conversation two ways. In his play, it looked like this: BEN: I found the treasure! ELLA: Show me! In his novel, the same talking looked like this: 'I found the treasure!' Ben shouted. 'Show me!' Ella replied. In the play, each speaker's name is printed right before the words they say. In the novel, quotation marks wrap around the spoken words instead. How is dialogue presented differently in a play versus a novel?
choices: In plays, the character's name appears before their lines; novels use quotation marks
questions.correct: In plays, the character's name appears before their lines; novels use quotation marks
questions.hint: Play: ROMEO: But soft! Novel: "But soft!" said Romeo.
questions.correct_feedback: Right! In plays the speaker's name comes before the lines, while novels wrap the spoken words in quotation marks.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, both plays and novels do have dialogue. Think about HOW each one shows who is speaking.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is that in plays the character's name appears before their lines, while novels use quotation marks. Both have dialogue, just shown differently.

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: Rosa's teacher showed the class a poem: 'The gentle breeze begins to blow, it lifts the kite and lets it go.' The words had a steady beat you could tap along to, and the ending words blow and go sounded alike. Then the teacher showed a regular story about a windy day, written in long sentences that flowed across the page with no steady beat and no matching end sounds. What structural element do poems often have that prose does not?
questions.hint: Poems often have rhythm (meter) and patterns of rhyming — prose usually doesn't.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The poem had a steady beat and rhyming end words, so poems often have meter and rhyme scheme that prose doesn't.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one. The poem had a steady tap-along beat and words that sounded alike at the ends. What are those two things called?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is meter and rhyme scheme. The steady beat is meter and the matching end sounds are the rhyme scheme.

questions.prompt: A poem is written in lines that are grouped together. What do we call a group of lines in a poem?
questions.hint: It's the poetry word for a bunch of lines that go together, like a paragraph but for poems.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A group of lines in a poem is called a stanza, kind of like a paragraph in a story.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. We're talking about poems here. Which word names a group of lines grouped together?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is a stanza. That's the name for a group of lines in a poem.

questions.prompt: A play (drama) is written so actors can perform it on a stage. Which of these is a type of writing MEANT to be acted out?
questions.hint: Think about which one has actors, a stage, and lines to say out loud.
questions.correct_feedback: Right! A drama, or play, is written so actors can perform it on a stage.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, which of these is written for actors to perform, not just to read or use for information?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is a drama, or play. It's the kind of writing meant to be acted out on a stage.

questions.prompt: Prose is regular writing, like a story or a novel. Prose is organized into what?
questions.hint: It's the same thing you break a story into when you start a new idea.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Prose, like stories and novels, is organized into paragraphs.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. Prose is regular story writing. Poems use stanzas, but what does prose group its sentences into?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is paragraphs. Prose organizes its sentences into paragraphs, while poems use stanzas.

questions.prompt: In a play, the words a character says out loud are called their lines. In a play, which piece of writing tells a character what to SAY?
questions.hint: It's the part the actor reads out loud when it's their turn to talk.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The words a character says out loud are their lines, also called dialogue.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, page numbers and covers don't tell a character what to say. Which part gives them their spoken words?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is their lines, also called dialogue. Those are the words a character speaks out loud in a play.

questions.prompt: One big difference between a poem and prose is how they look on the page. A poem is usually written in what?
questions.hint: Picture a poem: the words are stacked in little rows, not stretched all the way across the page.
questions.correct_feedback: Right! Poems are usually written in short lines, which makes them look different from prose on the page.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. Think about how a poem is shaped on the page compared to a long story. What does it use?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is short lines. Poems are usually written in short lines, unlike the long paragraphs of prose.

standards.standard_description: Compare and contrast the point of view from which different stories are narrated, including first- and third-person.
standards.parent_tip: Ask 'Is the character telling the story (first person) or is someone else describing it (third person)?'

questions.prompt: The sentence "I couldn't believe my eyes when I opened the door" is narrated from the character's own point of view. If this same event was instead told by a narrator who is *not* a character in the story, what would most likely change for the reader?
choices: The reader would know exactly what every character was thinking and feeling.
The reader would have to guess more about the character's inner thoughts based on their actions.
The reader would still know exactly what the character was thinking and feeling directly.
The reader would only know what the character did, not how they felt.
questions.correct: The reader would have to guess more about the character's inner thoughts based on their actions.
questions.hint: Think about how a story changes when a character tells it themselves compared to when someone outside the story tells it. What kind of information do you get directly from the character, and what do you have to figure out when a narrator describes them?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Without an inside narrator, the reader would have to guess more about the character's thoughts based on their actions.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. If the narrator is NOT inside the character's head, would we still get to know their private feelings directly?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is that the reader would have to guess more about the character's inner thoughts based on their actions. An outside narrator can't reveal private feelings directly.

questions.prompt: Read these two passages: Passage A: 'I pressed my face against the cold window, watching the first snowflakes fall. A shiver went down my spine, not from the cold, but from excitement! I couldn't wait to build a snowman.' Passage B: 'Leo pressed his face against the cold window, watching the first snowflakes fall. A shiver went down his spine, not from the cold, but from excitement! He couldn't wait to build a snowman.' How does the way each passage is told affect how you understand the main character's feelings?
choices: Both passages make you feel exactly the same way about the character's feelings.
Passage B gives more facts about the snow, and Passage A gives more facts about the window.
Passage A makes you feel like you are the character, while Passage B lets you watch the character from the outside.
Passage A describes more about the cold, and Passage B describes more about the excitement.
questions.correct: Passage A makes you feel like you are the character, while Passage B lets you watch the character from the outside.
questions.hint: Think about who is telling each story and how that makes you, the reader, feel connected to the character's thoughts and emotions.
questions.correct_feedback: Exactly! Passage A uses 'I,' so you feel like the character. Passage B uses 'he,' so you watch from the outside.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, notice Passage A says 'I' and Passage B says 'he.' How does each word change how close you feel to Leo?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is that Passage A makes you feel like you are the character, while Passage B lets you watch the character from the outside. The words 'I' and 'he' create that difference.

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: In Sam's story, the narrator says, 'I was so nervous I could barely breathe, but I had no idea what my sister Kate was thinking as she stared out the window.' Because Sam wrote using the word I, readers only learn what the narrator himself sees and feels. We never find out Kate's private thoughts, only what the narrator can observe from the outside. How does first-person narration affect a story compared to third-person?
choices: First person limits you to one character's perspective and feelings
Third person is always more honest
First person gives you access to every character's thoughts
questions.correct: First person limits you to one character's perspective and feelings
questions.hint: With 'I,' you only know what THAT character knows and feels.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Using 'I,' the narrator only knows his own thoughts and can't see Kate's, so first person limits you to one character's perspective.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. The narrator says 'I' and never learns Kate's private thoughts. What does that tell you about first person?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is that first person limits you to one character's perspective and feelings. The 'I' narrator can't know Kate's private thoughts.

questions.prompt: Read the story below:"Lily looked out the window. She felt a little nervous about her first day at the new school. 'What if no one wants to play with me?' she wondered. Her mom smiled, 'You'll do great, sweetie.' But Lily still worried about making new friends."Which statement best describes the point of view of this story?
choices: It is told by Lily, using words like "I" and "my."
It is told by Lily's mom, describing her own thoughts and feelings.
It is told by an outside narrator who focuses on one character's thoughts and feelings.
It is told by an outside narrator who knows what every character is thinking and feeling.
questions.correct: It is told by an outside narrator who focuses on one character's thoughts and feelings.
questions.hint: Look closely at whose thoughts and feelings the narrator shares in the story.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The narrator isn't Lily, but it shares only Lily's worries and feelings. That's an outside narrator zooming in on one character.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, look at whose thoughts we hear. Do we get inside everyone's head, or just one person's? And is that person telling the story?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is an outside narrator who focuses on one character. We hear only Lily's feelings, but the story uses 'she,' not 'I.'

questions.prompt:
The same event is described two ways: Version A: "I scored the winning goal and felt like a hero." Version B: "Jake scored the winning goal. His teammates cheered, but the other team walked away in silence."

How does the point of view change what the reader learns?
choices: Both versions give the same information
Version A shows Jake's feelings; Version B shows both teams' reactions
questions.correct: Version A shows Jake's feelings; Version B shows both teams' reactions
questions.hint: First person (A) gives inner feelings. Third person (B) gives a wider view of the scene.
questions.correct_feedback: Exactly! Version A only shows Jake's own feelings, while Version B stands back and shows how both teams react. Point of view changes what you see.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost. Reread both. One says 'I' and shares one person's feelings. The other watches from outside and shows more people. Do they really match?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is: Version A shows Jake's feelings; Version B shows both teams' reactions. The 'I' version stays inside Jake; the other sees everyone.

questions.prompt: Read this sentence: "I ran as fast as I could to catch the bus." Is this sentence told in first-person or third-person point of view?
choices: It has no point of view
questions.hint: Look for the word "I." When the narrator uses "I" and "me," the story is told in first-person.
questions.correct_feedback: Right! The word 'I' means the narrator is talking about themselves, so it's first-person point of view.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look at the special words. Do you see 'I' and 'my,' or names like 'he' and 'she'? That tells you the point of view.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is first-person. The word 'I' shows the narrator is a character telling their own story.

questions.prompt: Read this sentence: "Maria opened the box and smiled at what she saw." Is this sentence told in first-person or third-person point of view?
questions.hint: The narrator uses the character's name and the word "she" instead of "I." That is third-person.
questions.correct_feedback: Nice! It uses her name 'Maria' and the word 'she,' so an outside narrator is telling it. That's third-person.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try. Check the words carefully. Does the narrator say 'I,' or do they name Maria and say 'she'? That points to the answer.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is third-person. The story uses 'Maria' and 'she,' so the narrator is outside the story, not the character.

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: One story begins like this: 'I walked down to the pond and dipped my toes in the cool water.' The narrator is a character inside the story, telling it by talking about themselves with one special word. Which word tells you a story is being told in first-person point of view?
questions.hint: First-person means the narrator is telling their own story, so they use "I" and "me."
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The word 'I' shows the narrator is a character in the story, talking about themselves. That's first-person.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one. Which word means the narrator is talking about their own self, not about someone else? Say each choice aloud.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'I.' That special word shows the narrator is a character telling the story about themselves.

questions.prompt: A story begins: "He walked into the room and looked around." Which point of view is this story told from?
questions.hint: The word "He" tells you the narrator is talking about someone else, not themselves. That is third-person.
questions.correct_feedback: Great! The word 'He' means an outside narrator is telling about the character, not the character speaking. That's third-person.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet. Look at the very first word. Does it say 'I,' or does it name someone with 'he'? That decides the point of view.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is third-person. The story uses 'He,' so a narrator outside the story is telling it.

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: A teacher wrote three sentences on the board and asked her class to find the one where the writer is telling about themselves. She reminded them that first-person point of view uses the word I, which shows the narrator is a character in the story. Which sentence is told in first-person point of view?
choices: "They love the smell of fresh cookies."
"I love the smell of fresh cookies."
"Tom loves the smell of fresh cookies."
questions.correct: "I love the smell of fresh cookies."
questions.hint: First-person uses the word "I." Find the sentence where the narrator is talking about themselves.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'I love the smell of fresh cookies' uses 'I,' so the writer is telling about themselves. That's first-person.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. Which sentence uses the word 'I' to show the writer is talking about their own self? The others use names or 'they.'
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'I love the smell of fresh cookies.' The word 'I' shows the narrator is a character in the story.

standards.standard_description: Make connections between the text of a story or drama and a visual or oral presentation of the text.
standards.parent_tip: After reading a book, watch the movie version and discuss what's different and why.

questions.prompt: A student reads a chapter about a thunderstorm, then watches a movie version with dramatic music and dark lighting. The movie version adds —
choices: Mood and atmosphere through sound and visuals
questions.correct: Mood and atmosphere through sound and visuals
questions.hint: Music and lighting create feelings that words alone describe differently.
questions.correct_feedback: Exactly! The dramatic music and dark lighting create a feeling. Sound and visuals add mood and atmosphere the words alone couldn't give.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, think about what music and dark lighting do to you. Do they add new characters, or do they change how the scene feels?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is mood and atmosphere through sound and visuals. The music and dark lighting make you feel the storm, not just read it.

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: In the book, the narrator told readers, 'Deep down, Priya felt afraid, even though she smiled on the outside.' When Priya's class turned the book into a play, there was no narrator to share those private thoughts. The audience could only watch the actors and hear them speak, so Priya's hidden feelings were never told out loud. When a book is turned into a play, what might be LOST?
choices: The character's inner thoughts described by the narrator
questions.correct: The character's inner thoughts described by the narrator
questions.hint: On stage, you can't hear what a character is thinking the way a narrator can tell you.
questions.correct_feedback: Right! In a play there's no narrator, so Priya's hidden feelings can't be spoken. The character's inner thoughts get lost.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try. In a play you only see and hear the actors. Which part of the book did only the narrator tell? That's what disappears.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is the character's inner thoughts described by the narrator. On stage there's no narrator to reveal Priya's private feelings.

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: When Jordan read the book, the words simply said, 'Grandpa was upset.' But when Jordan watched the movie of the same story, he saw the actor's deep frown, heard the shaky, angry sound of his voice, and noticed how he crossed his arms tightly. The movie let Jordan see and hear things that the plain words on the page could not show. What might a visual presentation (movie or play) ADD to a story that the text cannot?
choices: Facial expressions, tone of voice, and body language of actors
questions.correct: Facial expressions, tone of voice, and body language of actors
questions.hint: Actors bring characters to life with their faces, voices, and movements.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The plain words just said 'upset,' but the movie showed the frown, voice, and crossed arms. Actors add expressions, tone, and body language.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost. Think about what Jordan could see and hear in the movie that words couldn't show. Was it more words, or was it the actor?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is facial expressions, tone of voice, and body language of actors. The movie showed Grandpa's frown and shaky voice that words couldn't.

questions.prompt: A book describes a character as "a tall woman with kind eyes." In the movie, the actress has a warm smile and speaks softly. How does the movie expand on the text?
choices: It shows specific visual and audio details that bring 'kind eyes' to life
questions.correct: It shows specific visual and audio details that bring 'kind eyes' to life
questions.hint: The movie translates 'kind eyes' into a warm smile and soft voice — making it real.
questions.correct_feedback: Nice! The book just says 'kind eyes,' but the movie shows a warm smile and soft voice. It adds details that bring the words to life.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one. Does the movie fight against the book, or does it show real sights and sounds that match and expand the words?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is: it shows specific visual and audio details that bring 'kind eyes' to life. The movie adds a smile and voice the text only hinted at.

questions.prompt: Imagine reading a book where a wizard's tower is described as being made of smooth, black stone, reaching high into the clouds, with a giant, glowing crystal at the very top. If the movie adaptation of this story shows the wizard's tower as a short, brick building with a regular antenna, what is the biggest difference a reader would notice between the book and the movie?
choices: The movie version has more exciting characters.
The overall message of the story is changed in the movie.
How the wizard's tower appears is very different in each version.
The movie tells the story much faster than the book.
questions.correct: How the wizard's tower appears is very different in each version.
questions.hint: Think about the specific details given for the wizard's tower in the book compared to the movie.
questions.correct_feedback: Exactly! The book's tall, glowing tower becomes a short brick building in the movie. The biggest change is how the tower looks.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. The question is about the tower. Compare the tall glowing tower to the short brick one. What's the clearest difference?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is: how the wizard's tower appears is very different in each version. The book's grand glowing tower became a plain short building.

questions.prompt: Maya read a book about a scary forest. Then she watched the movie of the same story. In the movie, she could HEAR the wolves howling and the wind blowing. What did the movie add that the book did not have?
questions.hint: Think about what your ears can do while watching a movie, but not while reading a book.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! In the book you only read words, but the movie let Maya hear the wolves and wind. The movie adds sound you can hear.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, think about the word HEAR. The book had words on a page. What new thing did the movie let Maya's ears do?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is sound you can hear. The movie let Maya hear the wolves howling and the wind, which a book cannot do.

questions.prompt: Ben read a play in his book. Then his class ACTED it out on a stage. In the acted-out version, real people said the lines out loud. What could Ben now SEE that he could not see in the book?
questions.hint: When a story is performed on a stage, who is up there saying the words?
questions.correct_feedback: Right! On the book page you just read the play, but on stage Ben saw real people moving and speaking. That's what acting adds.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try. Ben's class acted it out on a stage. What new thing could his eyes SEE that flat book pages could not show?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is real actors moving and speaking. On stage, live people bring the play to life in a way the book pages could not.

questions.prompt: A book describes a birthday party as 'colorful and happy.' In the movie of the story, the party scene uses bright colors and cheerful music. The movie and the book both show the party as —
questions.hint: The book says the party is happy. Do the bright colors and cheerful music match that?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The book called the party 'happy,' and the movie's bright colors and cheerful music feel happy too. They match!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet. The book's party is colorful, and the movie uses bright colors and cheerful music. What good feeling do BOTH show?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is happy. The book says the party is happy, and the movie's bright colors and cheerful music show the same happy feeling.

questions.prompt: Liam listened to an AUDIO version of a story instead of reading it. An actor read the words out loud in a soft, gentle voice. The audio version lets Liam —
choices: Taste the food in the story
questions.hint: An audio version is made for one of your senses. Which one?
questions.correct_feedback: Nice! Instead of reading, Liam listened. A gentle voice reading aloud lets him hear how the words sound.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. It's an AUDIO version, so an actor reads out loud. What does listening let Liam do that reading silently would not?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is hear how the words sound. An audio version reads the story aloud, so Liam listens instead of reading with his eyes.

questions.prompt: In the book, a character named Sam is described as tall with red hair. In the MOVIE, the actor playing Sam is tall with red hair. The movie version of Sam —
questions.hint: Compare the book's words to the actor. Are they the same or different?
questions.correct_feedback: Right! The book says Sam is tall with red hair, and the movie actor is tall with red hair too. The movie matches the book.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one. Compare them. The book says tall with red hair, and the actor is tall with red hair. Do they look the same or different?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is: it matches the book. Both the book Sam and the movie actor are tall with red hair, so they look the same.

standards.standard_description: Compare and contrast the treatment of similar themes and topics in stories, myths, and traditional literature from different cultures.
standards.parent_tip: Read myths from different cultures and discuss: 'How does this culture explain the same thing differently?'

questions.prompt: A Greek myth says the sun is pulled across the sky by the god Helios in a chariot. A Native American story says the sun is carried by a spirit eagle. Both stories try to explain —
choices: How the sun moves across the sky
questions.correct: How the sun moves across the sky
questions.hint: Both cultures created stories to explain the same natural phenomenon — the sun's movement.
questions.correct_feedback: Exactly! One story uses a chariot, the other an eagle, but both are trying to explain the same thing: how the sun moves across the sky.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, look past the chariot and the eagle. Both stories are about the sun doing what? Find the idea they share.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is how the sun moves across the sky. Both myths use different characters to explain that same sky event.

questions.prompt: Imagine you read two flood myths. In the first myth, a powerful god sends the flood because people have become unkind. In the second myth, the flood happens because a giant river overflows for many years without stopping. What is the main difference in how these two myths explain why the flood began?
choices: Both myths explain that water covered the entire land.
The first myth probably has a hero, and the second one might not.
The first myth shows the flood as a punishment, while the second shows it as a natural event.
One myth describes a powerful god, while the other does not.
questions.correct: The first myth shows the flood as a punishment, while the second shows it as a natural event.
questions.hint: Focus on the specific reasons given in each myth for why the flood started, not just what happened during the flood.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! In the first myth a god sends the flood as a punishment, but in the second it's just a river overflowing naturally. That's the key difference.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try. Ask WHY each flood began. One is caused on purpose by a god, the other happens on its own. How are those reasons different?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is: the first shows the flood as a punishment, the second as a natural event. One god acts on purpose; the other is just a river.

questions.prompt: In a European fairy tale, a small fox uses its wits to outsmart a much larger, stronger animal. In a West African folktale, Anansi the spider, who is not very big, uses clever plans to trick powerful creatures. What main idea do these two stories share?
choices: Both stories are about animals that live in forests.
Both stories teach that being smart can help a weaker character win.
The main characters in both stories are always kind.
Strong characters always get what they want in both tales.
questions.correct: Both stories teach that being smart can help a weaker character win.
questions.hint: Think about what the fox and Anansi *do* to the stronger characters and what happens as a result in both tales.
questions.correct_feedback: Right! A small fox and a small spider both beat bigger, stronger foes using their brains. The shared idea is that being smart helps the weaker one win.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost. Both main characters are small but still win. How do they beat the bigger animals? Think about what those two heroes share.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is: both teach that being smart can help a weaker character win. The little fox and little spider both outsmart stronger animals.

questions.prompt: Imagine you have read two stories from different cultures that both share the theme of 'courage.' What would be the *best* way to compare *how* each story shows courage?
choices: See if the stories are told by a narrator or by one of the characters.
Find out if the stories were written a long time ago or more recently.
Look at what brave actions the characters take and the results of those actions.
Count how many times the word "courage" is used in each story.
questions.correct: Look at what brave actions the characters take and the results of those actions.
questions.hint: The standard asks you to compare how different stories *treat* or show a theme; think about what makes a theme clear in a story.
questions.correct_feedback: Exactly! To compare HOW each story shows courage, you look at the brave actions the characters take and what happens because of them.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. Counting the word 'courage' or checking dates won't show HOW courage looks. What would you actually watch the characters do?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is: look at what brave actions the characters take and the results. That shows HOW each story pictures courage, not just the word.

questions.prompt: A Chinese myth explains why the moon changes shape each month. A Hawaiian myth also explains why the moon changes shape each month. What SAME topic do both myths explain?
questions.hint: Look at what BOTH myths are about. The first sentence and the second sentence say the same thing.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Both the Chinese and Hawaiian myths explain why the moon changes shape each month. Same topic, different cultures.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet. Both stories are about the same thing in the sky. Reread what each myth explains, and find the topic they share.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is why the moon changes shape. Both myths, from different cultures, explain that same thing about the moon.

questions.prompt: An African folktale teaches that being kind to others brings good things back to you. A Japanese folktale also teaches that being kind to others brings good things back to you. What lesson (theme) do both stories share?
questions.hint: Both folktales teach the SAME lesson. Read what each one 'teaches' and find the match.
questions.correct_feedback: Right! Both folktales teach that when you are kind to others, good things come back to you. That shared lesson is the theme.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try. A theme is the lesson a story teaches. Both stories teach the same lesson about how being kind works. What is it?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is kindness comes back to you. Both folktales teach that same lesson, so it's the theme they share.

questions.prompt: In a Greek myth, a clever hero uses a trick to escape a giant. In a West African tale, a clever spider uses a trick to escape a lion. How are these two stories ALIKE?
choices: Both take place under the ocean
Both are about the same real king
Both have a clever character who escapes danger with a trick
questions.correct: Both have a clever character who escapes danger with a trick
questions.hint: Compare the two heroes. What do the hero and the spider both DO to get away?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A clever hero and a clever spider both use a trick to escape danger. That's how the two stories are alike.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one. In both tales a smart character faces something dangerous. How do they get away? Find what both stories have in common.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is: both have a clever character who escapes danger with a trick. The hero and the spider both trick their way out of trouble.

questions.prompt: One myth says thunder happens because a giant is bowling in the sky. Another myth says thunder happens because a dragon is roaring. What is the SAME about these two myths?
choices: They both explain how to swim
They both explain why grass is green
They both are set in a school
They both try to explain what causes thunder
questions.correct: They both try to explain what causes thunder
questions.hint: The word 'thunder' shows up in both. Ask: what are both myths trying to explain?
questions.correct_feedback: Right! A bowling giant and a roaring dragon are different, but both myths explain the same thing: what causes thunder.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. Forget the giant and the dragon. What loud thing in the sky are both myths trying to explain?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is: they both try to explain what causes thunder. Different characters, but the same big question about that sky sound.

questions.prompt: A Norse myth has a brave hero who fights a monster to protect his village. A Mexican myth has a brave hero who fights a monster to protect her village. What do BOTH heroes have in common?
choices: Each is brave and fights a monster to protect the village
One is a teacher and one is a chef
One flies and the other swims
Neither one is brave at all
questions.correct: Each is brave and fights a monster to protect the village
questions.hint: Both sentences describe the hero the same way. Look for the words that repeat: brave, monster, protect the village.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Both heroes are brave and fight a monster to protect their village. That's exactly what they have in common.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet. One hero is Norse and one is Mexican, but they do the same brave thing. What do both heroes do for their village?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is: each is brave and fights a monster to protect the village. Both heroes share that same courageous action.

standards.standard_description: By end of the year, read and comprehend literature in the grades 4-5 text complexity band proficiently.
standards.parent_tip: Encourage your child to read chapter books independently and discuss what they've read.

questions.prompt:
Nobody knew where the music came from. Every night at midnight, a violin played from somewhere deep in the woods. The townspeople locked their doors and whispered about ghosts. Only young Alma grabbed her lantern and followed the sound.

What does Alma's action reveal about her?
choices: She's curious and braver than the other townspeople
questions.correct: She's curious and braver than the other townspeople
questions.hint: While everyone else locked their doors in fear, Alma went TOWARD the mystery.
questions.correct_feedback: Exactly! Everyone else locked their doors, but Alma grabbed a lantern and followed the scary sound. That shows she's curious and brave.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, compare Alma to the townspeople. They hid, but she went toward the mystery. What does that choice tell you about her?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is: she's curious and braver than the other townspeople. While they locked their doors, Alma chased the mystery with her lantern.

questions.prompt:
The treaty was signed on a Tuesday. By Wednesday, the two kingdoms had already broken it. **Peace,** the old queen muttered, "is harder to keep than to promise."

What is the queen's message about peace?
choices: Making peace is simple but maintaining it is the real challenge
questions.correct: Making peace is simple but maintaining it is the real challenge
questions.hint: They signed peace easily but broke it immediately — keeping peace is the hard part.
questions.correct_feedback: Right! The treaty was signed fast but broken the next day. The queen means peace is easy to promise but hard to keep going.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try. The peace was promised quickly, then fell apart. What is the queen really saying about the hard part of peace?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is: making peace is simple but maintaining it is the real challenge. The treaty was easy to sign but broke the very next day.

questions.prompt:
Theo didn't notice the first leaf fall. Or the second. But by the time the maple tree was half bare, he understood what his grandmother meant when she said, "Change happens slowly, then all at once."

What is Grandmother's saying about?
choices: How change in life can be gradual then suddenly obvious
questions.correct: How change in life can be gradual then suddenly obvious
questions.hint: The leaves are a metaphor — change builds quietly until suddenly you notice everything is different.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Theo missed the first leaves, then suddenly the tree was bare. Grandma means change can be slow, then all at once obvious.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost. Grandma's saying isn't really about maple trees. Think about how the slow, then sudden change could fit life too. What's her bigger point?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is: how change in life can be gradual then suddenly obvious. The falling leaves are just an example of that bigger idea.

questions.prompt:
Zuri had two rules: never break a promise, and always carry a pencil. **You never know,** she said, "when you'll need to write something down before you forget it — or before someone tries to make you forget."

What does the second rule suggest about Zuri?
choices: She values truth and recording important things
questions.correct: She values truth and recording important things
questions.hint: She writes things down so they can't be changed or forgotten — she values accuracy and truth.
questions.correct_feedback: Right! Zuri always carries a pencil so she can write down the truth before it's forgotten. She values truth and recording what matters.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. Why would Zuri always carry a pencil to write things down, even if someone wants her to forget? What does that show she cares about?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is: she values truth and recording important things. Her pencil rule shows she wants to keep the truth safe from being forgotten.

questions.prompt:
The ship had been at sea for thirty-seven days. Fresh water was running low, and the crew whispered about turning back. Captain Adaeze stood at the bow, eyes fixed on the horizon. **Land by sunrise,** she said. Nobody believed her. But at dawn, there it was.

What theme does this passage suggest?
choices: Faith and determination can lead you through doubt
questions.correct: Faith and determination can lead you through doubt
questions.hint: Everyone doubted, but the captain held firm — and she was right.
questions.correct_feedback: Exactly! No one believed the captain, but she trusted herself and was right at dawn. The theme is that faith and determination guide you through doubt.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet. Think about the captain standing firm while others doubted, and land appearing at dawn. What lesson does her belief teach?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is: faith and determination can lead you through doubt. The captain believed against everyone's doubt, and land appeared at sunrise.

questions.prompt: Maya had never seen the ocean before. When the waves finally came into view, she gasped and pressed her face to the car window. "It's even bigger than I imagined!" she said. How does Maya feel when she sees the ocean?
questions.hint: Look at what Maya does and says. Gasping and saying it's bigger than she imagined tells you her feeling.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Maya gasped and said the ocean was bigger than she imagined. Those clues show she feels amazed and excited.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look at what Maya does. She gasps and presses her face to the window. Do those actions sound bored, or full of wonder?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is amazed and excited. Maya gasps and says the ocean is bigger than she imagined, which shows her wonder.

questions.prompt: The old lighthouse stood at the edge of the cliff. Its light had guided ships safely to shore for over one hundred years. Where does this story take place?
choices: At a lighthouse by the sea
questions.correct: At a lighthouse by the sea
questions.hint: The passage names the place directly. A lighthouse on a cliff guides ships to shore.
questions.correct_feedback: Right! The story talks about a lighthouse on a cliff guiding ships to shore. It takes place at a lighthouse by the sea.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try. Look at the clue words: a lighthouse, a cliff, and ships. What kind of place do those details point to?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is at a lighthouse by the sea. The story describes a lighthouse on a cliff guiding ships to shore.

questions.prompt: Ben studied for the spelling test all week. When he got his paper back, every single word was correct. He jumped up with a huge smile. Why is Ben happy?
questions.hint: The sentence right before his smile tells you why. Every word was correct.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Ben studied all week and every word was correct, so he jumped up smiling. He's happy because he got every word right.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet. Reread what happened. Ben studied hard, and his paper came back with every word a certain way. Why the big smile?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is: he got every word right. Ben studied all week, and every spelling word was correct, so he cheered.

questions.prompt: The storm grew worse. Rain slammed against the windows, and thunder shook the little house. The children pulled their blankets tighter and stayed close together. What is happening in the story?
choices: A sunny day at the beach
A quiet trip to the library
questions.hint: Notice the rain, thunder, and the children pulling their blankets tighter. What kind of weather is it?
questions.correct_feedback: Right! Rain slams the windows, thunder shakes the house, and the children huddle together. It's a scary storm at night.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look at the clues: rain, thunder, and kids pulling blankets tight. Does that sound sunny, or like something else?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is a scary storm at night. The rain, thunder, and frightened children all point to a nighttime storm.

questions.prompt: Grandpa handed Lily an old, worn baseball glove. "This was mine when I was your age," he said softly. "I want you to have it now." Why does Grandpa give Lily the glove?
choices: Because he wants to share something special with her
Because he does not like it
questions.correct: Because he wants to share something special with her
questions.hint: Grandpa says it was his and speaks softly. Giving something from his own childhood shows it means a lot to him.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Grandpa says the glove was his and softly gives it to Lily. He wants to share something special with her.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try. Grandpa gives Lily his own old glove and speaks softly. Does that sound like he dislikes it, or that it means a lot?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is: because he wants to share something special with her. The glove was his as a boy, and he passes it on with love.

standards.standard_description: Refer to details and examples in a text when explaining what the text says explicitly and when drawing inferences.
standards.parent_tip: Teach your child to say 'The text says...' when supporting their answers with evidence.

questions.prompt:
The International Space Station orbits Earth at approximately 17,500 miles per hour. At this speed, astronauts experience 16 sunrises and 16 sunsets every 24 hours.

What detail explains why astronauts see so many sunrises?
choices: The sun moves faster in space
It orbits at 17,500 mph, completing an orbit roughly every 90 minutes
questions.correct: It orbits at 17,500 mph, completing an orbit roughly every 90 minutes
questions.hint: The extreme speed means it circles Earth many times a day — each orbit brings a new sunrise.
questions.correct_feedback: Exactly! Zooming around at 17,500 mph, the station circles Earth about every 90 minutes, so astronauts see a sunrise each time around.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, the sun doesn't really move faster. Look for the detail about how fast the station travels and how often it circles Earth.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is: it orbits at 17,500 mph, completing an orbit roughly every 90 minutes. Each fast trip around brings another sunrise.

questions.prompt:
Jane Goodall arrived in Tanzania in 1960 with no formal training in science. Within months, she observed chimpanzees using sticks as tools to extract termites — a discovery that changed our understanding of what makes humans unique.

What inference can you make about why this discovery was so important?
choices: Scientists previously believed only humans used tools
People didn't know about termites before
questions.correct: Scientists previously believed only humans used tools
questions.hint: The text says it 'changed our understanding of what makes humans unique' — tool use was thought to be a human-only ability.
questions.correct_feedback: Right! The passage says this changed what makes humans unique, meaning people once thought only humans used tools. That's why it mattered.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try. The text says the discovery changed what makes humans 'unique.' What did people probably believe about tools before Goodall saw the chimps?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is: scientists previously believed only humans used tools. Seeing chimps use sticks changed that idea, which made it important.

questions.prompt:
Honeybees communicate through a "waggle dance." A bee returning from a food source dances in a figure-eight pattern. The angle of the dance indicates the direction of the food relative to the sun.

What specific detail tells you HOW bees show direction?
choices: They fly in a straight line
The angle of the dance relative to the sun
questions.correct: The angle of the dance relative to the sun
questions.hint: The last sentence gives the precise detail about direction.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The passage says the angle of the dance shows the food's direction compared to the sun. That's the detail about HOW bees point the way.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. Buzzing and circles aren't in the passage. Which detail tells the exact way bees show which direction food is?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is the angle of the dance relative to the sun. The passage says that angle points to the food's direction.

questions.prompt:
During the California Gold Rush of 1849, most miners didn't get rich from gold. However, merchants who sold pickaxes, shovels, and jeans to the miners made fortunes.

What can you infer from this passage?
choices: The Gold Rush lasted one year
Selling supplies to miners was more profitable than mining itself
