questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try. A comma often goes before a joining word that connects two full ideas. Where does the first idea end?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is after 'lazily.' The comma marks the end of the first idea before 'and then it jumped.'

questions.prompt: How do you write a book title?
questions.hint: Most words in a title get capitalized!
questions.correct_feedback: Right! In a title we capitalize the important words, like the big and meaningful ones.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, titles aren't all lowercase or all uppercase. Think about which words in a title get a capital letter.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'Capitalize the important words.' Titles capitalize the main, meaningful words, not every letter.

questions.prompt: Which is correctly punctuated dialogue?
questions.hint: The comma goes inside the quotation marks!
questions.correct_feedback: Perfect! The exact spoken words go inside the quotation marks, with a comma before 'she said.'
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one. Quotation marks wrap only the words a person actually speaks. Check where those marks belong.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is '"Hello," she said.' The quotation marks go around only the spoken word, with a comma inside.

questions.prompt: Which is the correct possessive: That is the ___ ball.
questions.hint: Apostrophe + S shows one dog owns it!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Dog's' with an apostrophe shows the ball belongs to the dog. That's a possessive.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost. To show that the ball belongs to one dog, you need a special mark. Which spelling shows ownership?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'dog's.' The apostrophe before the s shows the ball belongs to one dog.

questions.prompt: Which sentence has the correct end punctuation? Pick the one that asks a question.
questions.hint: A question needs a question mark at the end.
questions.correct_feedback: That's right! A question ends with a question mark, and it also starts with a capital letter.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. A sentence that asks something needs a special ending mark and a capital start. Which one has both?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'Where is my backpack?' It's a question, so it ends with a question mark and starts with a capital.

questions.prompt: Every sentence should begin with what kind of letter? "___he dog ran fast."
questions.hint: The very first letter of a sentence is always capitalized.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Every sentence begins with a capital letter to show a new idea is starting.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try. Think about how the very first word of any sentence should look. What kind of letter starts it off?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is a capital letter. Every sentence begins with one to show a new idea is starting.

questions.prompt: A person's name, like the one below, should always start with a capital letter. Which is written correctly?
questions.hint: Names of people begin with one capital letter, then lowercase.
questions.correct_feedback: Right! A person's name starts with one capital letter, so 'Maria' is written correctly.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, a name isn't all capitals or all lowercase. Which one has just the first letter capitalized?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'Maria.' A name gets a capital on the first letter only, not every letter.

questions.prompt: Where does the comma belong? "I bought apples ___ bananas, and grapes."
questions.hint: When you list three or more things, put a comma between each item.
questions.correct_feedback: Nice! When we list three things, we put a comma after each one, so 'apples' gets a comma too.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost. When you list things in a row, each item needs a comma between it and the next. Where's the first one missing?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is after 'apples.' In a list, each item gets a comma, so it goes after the first item.

standards.standard_description: Use knowledge of language and its conventions when writing, speaking, reading, or listening
standards.parent_tip: Match style to audience. Vary sentence length. Use precise words.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is most precise?
choices: The dog went over the grass.
The animal moved on the field.
The puppy bounded across the meadow.
questions.correct: The puppy bounded across the meadow.
questions.hint: Precise words paint a clearer picture!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'The puppy bounded across the meadow' uses exact words that paint a clear, lively picture.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try. Precise writing uses exact words instead of vague ones like 'thing' or 'stuff.' Which one shows the most?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'The puppy bounded across the meadow.' Its exact words paint the clearest, most precise picture.

questions.prompt: What is **style** in writing?
questions.hint: Style is the author's unique voice!
questions.correct_feedback: Exactly! Style is the special way an author writes, like their own writing voice.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet. Style isn't about paper, ink, or font. Think about how an author's writing itself sounds and feels.
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'the way an author writes.' Style is an author's own special way of putting words together.

questions.prompt: Why might a writer use a short, snappy sentence?
choices: because they ran out of words
questions.hint: Short sentences hit hard!
questions.correct_feedback: Right! A short, snappy sentence grabs attention and adds punch, giving an idea real impact.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one. Writers make choices on purpose. Why would a writer pick a short sentence to make an idea stand out?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'for emphasis or impact.' Short, snappy sentences make an idea stand out and hit harder.

questions.prompt: Match the style: writing to a friend versus writing a book report. The friend version should be:
questions.hint: Friends like casual!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Writing to a friend can be more casual and relaxed, like how you really talk together.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, think about how you write to a friend versus a teacher. A friendly note can be more relaxed. Which word fits?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'more casual.' Writing to a friend can be relaxed and friendly, not stiff and formal.

questions.prompt: Which word adds the strongest detail in: She felt ___?
questions.hint: Thrilled is more vivid than good!
questions.correct_feedback: Great! 'Thrilled' is a strong, exact word that shows a big, exciting feeling.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. Words like 'good' or 'okay' are pretty plain. Which word shows the strongest, biggest feeling?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'thrilled.' It's the strongest word, showing a big, exciting feeling instead of a plain one.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses the most exact, colorful word for how the boy moved?
choices: The boy raced down the street.
The boy went down the street.
The boy was on the street.
questions.correct: The boy raced down the street.
questions.hint: Which word tells you EXACTLY how the boy moved? "Raced" paints a clear picture.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Raced' is an exact, colorful word that shows exactly how fast the boy moved.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost. Words like 'went' or 'did' are pretty plain. Which one shows exactly how the boy moved down the street?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'The boy raced down the street.' 'Raced' is the exact, colorful word for how he moved.

questions.prompt: A good writer picks words that paint a clear picture. Which word paints the clearest picture of a happy dog?
questions.hint: Three of these words are plain. Which one really shows a feeling?
questions.correct_feedback: Right! 'Joyful' paints a clear, bright picture of a truly happy dog.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try. Words like 'fine' or 'nice' are kind of flat. Which word paints the clearest picture of a happy dog?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'joyful.' It paints the clearest, brightest picture of a very happy dog.

questions.prompt: Read this sentence: "The tiny kitten napped in the warm sunlight." Which words help you picture the scene?
questions.hint: Look for the describing words that help you SEE and FEEL the picture.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Tiny' and 'warm' are describing words that help you picture the little kitten and the sunlight.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one. Some of these are just small words like 'the' or 'a.' Which words actually describe and help you see the scene?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'tiny, warm.' Those describing words help you picture the little kitten in the sunlight.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is written in a clearer, more exact way?
choices: The stuff was in the place.
The books were on the shelf.
questions.correct: The books were on the shelf.
questions.hint: Which sentence tells you exactly WHAT and WHERE? The others are vague.
questions.correct_feedback: Nice! 'The books were on the shelf' tells us exactly what and where, with no vague words.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, most of these are full of fuzzy words like 'things,' 'stuff,' and 'someplace.' Which one is clear and exact?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'The books were on the shelf.' It's clear and exact, with no vague words like 'stuff' or 'things.'

questions.prompt: When a writer wants to describe a very loud noise, which word is the best, most exact choice?
questions.hint: Which word actually sounds loud? Say them out loud and listen.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Crashed' is the exact word for a very loud noise. You can almost hear it!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet. Words like 'walked' or 'said' aren't about noise at all. Which word sounds like a big, loud crash?
questions.reveal_feedback: The answer is 'crashed.' It's the exact word for a very loud noise, one you can almost hear.
